subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
title: AC Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
body:
**Key points from solved questions** -
$$ \mathrm{R}=2\Omega,\mathrm{R}_{1}5\Omega,\mathrm{R}_{2}=3\Omega $$
$$ I=\frac{E}{R+R_{2}}=\frac{10}{2+3}=\frac{10}{5}=2\ A $$ ##### Related Theory In a steady state DC circuit, the capacitor is fully charged and acts as an open circuit. - Given: $$ \mathrm{V}_{r m s}=210\mathrm{V}, $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{V}_{0}&=210\sqrt{2}\mathrm{~V}\\&\mathrm{C}=10\mu\mathrm{F}\end{aligned} $$
210 V, 50 Hz $$ \mathbf{V}_{0}=\mathbf{I}_{0}\mathbf{X}_{c} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=\mathrm{I}_{0}\times\frac{1}{\omega\mathrm{C}} $$ $$ V_{0}=1_{0}\times\frac{1}{2\pi\times50\times10\times10^{-6}} $$ $$ l_{0}=210\sqrt{2}\times2\pi\times500\times10^{-6} $$ $$ l_{0}=0.93A $$ - Capacitive inductance. $$ X_{C}=\frac{1}{2\pi fC} $$ As frequency decreases, $X_{C}$ increases
So, conductive current decreases. Hence, displacement current also decreases. - From the question. $$ \mathrm{C}=40\mu\mathrm{F}=40\times10^{-6}\mathrm{~F},\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=200\mathrm{~V},f=50\mathrm{~Hz} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}X_{C}&=\frac{1}{2\pi f C}=\frac{1}{2\pi\times50\times40\times10^{-6}}\\&=\frac{10^{6}}{100\pi\times40}=\frac{250}{\pi}\Omega\end{aligned} $$ $$ I_{rms}=\frac{V_{rms}}{X_{C}}=\frac{200}{\underbrace{250}_{\pi}}=2.5A. $$ - We know that, Impedance of R-C circuit. $$ Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{c}^{2}} $$ Here $$ R=100\ \Omega,X_{C}=100\ \Omega $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ Z=\sqrt{(100)^{2}+(100)^{2}}=100\sqrt{2}\Omega $$ At 50% charged, $ V_{max} = 220\sqrt{2} $ V Peak value of current, $$ I_{max}=\frac{V_{max}}{Z}=\frac{220\sqrt{2}}{100\sqrt{2}}=2.2A $$ - When an AC signal is coupled to a capacitor with capacitance C, the circuit's reactance is simply capacitive. We have, the capacitive reactance. $$ X_{C}=\frac{1}{\omega C}=\frac{1}{2\pi f C} $$ $$ x_{c}\propto\frac{1}{fC} $$ Considering two different cases of frequency and capacitance, we have $$ \frac{X^{\prime}}{X}=\frac{fC}{f^{\prime}C^{\prime}}=\frac{f\times C}{2f\times2C} $$ $$ [\because f^{\prime}=2f\text{and}C^{\prime}=2C] $$ $$ \frac{X^{\prime}}{X}=\frac{1}{4} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}X^{\prime}=\frac{X}{4}\end{aligned} $$

subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Resistor
title: AC Voltage Applied to a Resistor
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- We know that, $ V_{rms} = \frac{V_{o}}{\sqrt{2}} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ V_{0}=\sqrt{2}V_{rms} $

subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
title: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - $$ As \quad Q=Q_{o}e^{-1\frac{R.t}{2L}} $$ $$ \mathrm{Given:}R=1.5\Omega,:=12\mathrm{~mH} $$ $$ =12\times10^{-3}H $$ $$ Q=0.5Q_{o} $$ $$ 0.5Q_{0}=Q_{0}e^{-1\frac{R.t}{2L}} $$ $$ \ln(0.5)=-\frac{R.t}{2L} $$ $$ \ln(0.5)=-\frac{1.5\times t}{2\times0.012} $$ $$ \ln(0.5)=-62.5\times t $$ $$ t=\frac{\ln(0.5)}{-62.5} $$ $$ t=0.0111s\approx11.09ms $$ - Given, L = 10 mH = $ 10 \times 10^{-3} $ H $$ C=1\mu F=1\times10^{-6}F $$ $$ \mathrm{R=100\Omega} $$ Resonance frequency, $$ \begin{aligned}f_{0}&=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{LC}}}\\&=\frac{1}{2\times3.14}\sqrt{\frac{1}{10\times10^{-3}\times10^{-6}}}\\&=\frac{1}{2\times3.14}\sqrt{\frac{10^{8}}{1}}\\&=\frac{10^{4}}{2\times3.14}=1592.3\mathrm{~Hz}=1.59\mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned} $$ - Here: $$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{50}{\pi}\mathrm{~mH}=\frac{50}{\pi}\times10^{-3}\mathrm{~H} $$ Then, $$ \begin{aligned}f&=50\ Hz\\ X_{L}&=2\pi f L\\&=2\pi\times50\times\frac{50}{\pi}\times10^{-3}\\&=5\ \Omega\end{aligned} $$ Similarly, $$ X_{C}=\frac{1}{2\pi fC} $$ $$ X_{C}=\frac{1}{2\pi\times50\times\frac{10^{3}}{\pi}\times10^{-6}}=10\ \Omega $$ As impedance. $$ Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+(X_{C}-X_{L})^{2}} $$ $$ Z=\sqrt{(10)^{2}+(10-5)^{2}} $$ $$ =5\sqrt{5}\Omega $$ - Given. $$ \begin{aligned}&C=10\mu F,R=50\Omega,L=10H\\&\omega=2\pi f\\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{v}=\frac{\omega}{2\pi}=\frac{100}{2\pi}=\frac{50}{\pi}\mathrm{Hz} $$ $$ \begin{align*}Resonance~frequency,~v_{0}&=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}\\&=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10\times10\times10^{-6}}}\\&=\frac{100}{2\pi}\\&=50\end{align*} $$ -
Given. $$ \begin{array}{c}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L}}=40\mathrm{V}\\\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=10\mathrm{V}\\\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{R}}=40\mathrm{V}\\\mathrm{I}_{0}=10\sqrt{2}\end{array} $$ We know that. $$ \mathrm{V_{rms}=\sqrt{V_{R}^{2}+(V_{L}-V_{C})^{2}}} $$ ： $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{1600+900}=50\mathrm{~V} $$ $ \therefore $ $$ I_{rms}=\frac{I_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{10\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}=10\ A $$ $$ Z=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}}=\frac{50}{10}=5\Omega $$
##### Mnemonics LEI the CIE For an inductive [L] circuit, the EMF(E) is ahead of the current (I). While for a capacitive circuit (C), the current (I) is ahead of EMF (E) - For AC source, Given. $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=12\mathrm{~V},\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=0.2\mathrm{~A} $$ Impedance, $$ Z=\frac{V_{rms}}{I_{rms}}=\frac{12}{0.2}=60\Omega $$ and for DC source, Given. $$ V=12V,I=0.4A $$ Resistance. $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{12}{0.4}=30\ \Omega $$ Since, Z > R, we conclude there must be another component in the series, which is inductor. ∴ It is a series LR circuit. - Just after the switch is closed, the inductor behaves as open circuit and the capacitor behaves short circuit. The equivalent circuit of the situation is shown below. Now, these two resistors are in parallel.
$$ \begin{array}{c} \mathrm{R}_{eq}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}.\mathrm{R}}{2\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}=\frac{9}{2}\Omega\\\Rightarrow\quad\text{Current}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_{eq}}=\frac{18}{\frac{9}{2}}=4\mathrm{~A} \end{array} $$ - When capacitor is filled with mica, then capacitance C increases, so $X_{C}$ decreases. In case (2), $X_{C}$ decreases, so voltage across capacitor decreases. $$ \mathbf{V}_{1}>\mathbf{V}_{2} $$ - According to the question $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{R}&=30\Omega\\\mathrm{X_{L}}&=20\Omega\\f&=50\mathrm{Hz}\\\mathrm{X_{L}}&=\omega\mathrm{L}=2\pi\mathrm{fL}\\20&=2\pi\times50\times\mathrm{L}\end{aligned} $$ We know when frequency of AC source in changed to 100 Hz $$ X_{L}^{\prime}=\omega^{\prime}L=2\pi\times100\times L $$ $$ \mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^{\prime}=2\pi\times50\times2\times\mathrm{L} $$ from eq. (i), we get $$ X_{L}^{^{\prime}}=20\times2=40\ \Omega $$ Impedance $$ \begin{aligned}Z&=\sqrt{R^{2}+(X_{L}^{^{\prime}})^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{(30)^{2}+(40)^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{900+1600}\end{aligned} $$ $$ Z=50\ \Omega $$ Current, $$ I=\frac{V}{Z}=\frac{200}{50}=4A $$ - We know that $$ \tan\phi=\frac{\mathrm{X_{L}}}{\mathrm{R}} $$ and $$ \phi=\frac{3}{3} $$ $ \tan \phi = 1 $ $$ \phi=45^{\circ} $$ $$ \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}\mathrm{~r a d} $$ - For series LCR circuit. Voltage. $$ \mathbf{V}=\sqrt{\mathbf{V}_{R}^{2}+(\mathbf{V}_{L}-\mathbf{V}_{C})^{2}} $$ since $$ \mathbf{V}_{L}=\mathbf{V}_{C} $$ Hence, $$ \mathbf{V}=\mathbf{V}_{R}=220\mathrm{~V} $$ ∴ $$ I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{220}{100}=2.2A $$
##### Related Theory Series LCR circuit offers minimum impedance at resonance and current at this frequency is maximum. - According to question, current is maximum at resonance $$ \begin{aligned}\omega^{2}&=\frac{1}{\mathrm{LC}}\\\mathrm{L}&=\frac{1}{\omega^{2}\mathrm{C}}\\&=\frac{1}{(1000)^{2}(10\times10^{-6})}\\&=0.1\mathrm{~H}=100\mathrm{~mH}\end{aligned} $$
##### Related Theory For resonance to occur in LCR-Circuit, the presence of both capacitor (C) and inductor (L) is necessary. Resonance cannot be obtained in LR and LC circuit. - Phase difference between voltage and current is $$ \begin{array}{c}\tan\phi=\frac{\mathrm{X_{C}}-\mathrm{X_{L}}}{\mathrm{R}}\\\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\frac{1}{\omega\mathrm{C}}-\omega\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{R}}\\\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{R}=\frac{1}{\omega\mathrm{C}}-\omega\mathrm{L}\ $ \mathrm{R}+2\pi f\mathrm{L})=\frac{1}{2\pi f\mathrm{C}}\\\text{or}\quad\mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{2\pi f(\mathrm{R}+2\pi f\mathrm{L})}\end{array} $$ - According to the question, Time constant for LR circuit is, $ \tau = \frac{L}{R} $ $$ \tau=\frac{40}{8}=5s $$ ：

subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to an Inductor
title: AC Voltage Applied to an Inductor
summary: The diagram is shown in the figure.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{L}\frac{d\mathrm{I}}{dt} $$ The current in the circuit is $$ I\left(t\right)=I_{0}\sin\omega t $$ $$ \frac{d\mathrm{I}}{dt}=\frac{d}{dt}\mathrm{(I_{0}\sin\omega t)} $$ $$ =\mathrm{I}_{0}\omega\cos(\omega t) $$ $$ \mathrm{V_{L}}=\mathrm{L}\frac{d\mathrm{I}}{dt}=\mathrm{L}.\mathrm{I_{0}}\omega\cos\left(\omega t\right) $$ $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{L}\mathbf{I}_{0}\boldsymbol{\omega}\cos\left(\omega t\right) $$ - The diagram is shown in the figure. According to the question, the key is closed and when the iron rod is inserted inside the inductor, then inductance (L) of the coil increases.
When L increases, Then inductive reactance $ X_{L} = \omega L $ also increases. When $ X_{L} $ increases, Then current I decreases which is confirmed from the equation. $$ I=\frac{e}{X_{L}} $$ Since, $ X_{L} $ increases, hence I decreases and the glow of the bulb also decreases. - We know that, $$ Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{L}^{2}}=\sqrt{R^{2}+(2\pi fL)^{2}} $$ and $$ I=\frac{\mathbf{V}}{Z},\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I}^{2}\mathrm{R} $$ When an iron rod is inserted in the coil, self-inductance increases and the current decreases. Hence, the brightness of the bulb decreases.

title: Objective I
summary: In an AC circuit, instantaneous power $= vi$.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- RMS (root mean square) current: $$i_{\rm rms} = \sqrt{\langle i^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T i_0^2\sin^2\omega t\,dt} = i_0\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{i_0}{\sqrt{2}}$$ Similarly $V_{\rm rms} = V_0/\sqrt{2}$. The RMS value is what produces the same heating effect as an equivalent DC current. Answer: (B).
- In an AC circuit, instantaneous power $= vi$. The average power dissipated (only in resistors) is: $$P = V_{\rm rms} I_{\rm rms} \cos\phi$$ where $\phi$ is the phase angle between $V$ and $I$, and $\cos\phi = R/Z$ is the **power factor**. A pure inductor or capacitor has $\phi = 90°$, so $\cos\phi = 0$ and $P = 0$ — no average power is dissipated. Answer: (B).
- Resonance occurs when $X_L = X_C$ (i.e., $\omega L = 1/\omega C$), so the net reactance is zero. The impedance: $$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2} = \sqrt{R^2 + 0} = R$$ At resonance, $Z = R$ (minimum), current is maximum ($I = V/R$), and the circuit is purely resistive. The resonant frequency: $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$. Answer: (B).
- $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$. The inductive reactance increases with frequency — at high frequency an inductor effectively blocks the current. At DC ($f = 0$), $X_L = 0$ (inductor acts as a short circuit). Answer: (B).
- $X_C = 1/(\omega C) = 1/(2\pi fC)$. Capacitive reactance **decreases** with frequency — at high frequency a capacitor passes current easily (low impedance). At DC ($f = 0$), $X_C \to \infty$ (capacitor blocks DC). Answer: (B).
- An ideal transformer conserves power: $V_1 I_1 = V_2 I_2$. $$\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{1}{10} \implies I_2 = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5\,\rm A$$ Step-up in voltage → step-down in current (by the same factor). Answer: (B).
- For a pure inductor: $v = L\,di/dt$. If $v = V_0\sin\omega t$, then $i = -(V_0/\omega L)\cos\omega t = (V_0/\omega L)\sin(\omega t - 90°)$. Current **lags** voltage by 90°. Mnemonic: **ELI** — in an inductor (L), E leads I. Answer: (B).

formulas.latex: i_{\rm rms} = \sqrt{\langle i^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T i_0^2\sin^2\omega t\,dt} = i_0\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{i_0}{\sqrt{2}}

title: Objective II
summary: At resonance ($\omega = \omega_0$, $X_L = X_C$): - **A** ✓: $I_{\rm max} = V_0/R$ (maximum since $Z = R$ is minimum).
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- - **A** ✓: Inductor: $\phi = 90°$, $\cos\phi = 0$, $P = 0$. - **B** ✓: Capacitor: $\phi = -90°$, $\cos\phi = 0$, $P = 0$. - **C** ✓: Pure resistor: $\phi = 0°$, $\cos\phi = 1$, $P = I_{\rm rms}^2 R$. - **D** ✓: At resonance, $Z = R$, $\phi = 0°$, $\cos\phi = 1$, so the circuit behaves purely resistively. Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D).
- At resonance ($\omega = \omega_0$, $X_L = X_C$): - **A** ✓: $I_{\rm max} = V_0/R$ (maximum since $Z = R$ is minimum). - **B** ✓: $P = I_{\rm rms}^2 R$ — maximum current → maximum power in $R$. - **C** ✓: Voltage across $L$: $V_L = I\cdot X_L = (V_0/R)\cdot\omega_0 L$. For high $Q = \omega_0 L/R$, $V_L = QV_0 \gg V_0$. Similarly $V_C = QV_0$. These can greatly exceed the supply voltage (but $V_L$ and $V_C$ cancel each other). ✓ - **D** ✗: Impedance **decreases** to its minimum value $R$ at resonance. Answer: (A), (B), (C).

subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
title: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Given, $$ \begin{aligned}&L=5\ H\\&C=80\ \mu F\\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=40\ \Omega $$ $$ V=230V $$ Let the frequencies be, $$ \omega^{\prime}=\omega_{R}\pm\Delta\omega $$ where, ∴ $$ \Delta\omega=\frac{R}{2L} $$ $$ \Delta\omega=\frac{40}{2\times5}=4rad/s $$ And $$ \omega_{R}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}=50rad/s $$ $\therefore$ Frequencies are 50 - 4 and 50 + 4 = 46 rad/s and 54 rad/s -
For LCR circuit phase difference, $$ \phi=\tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathbf{X}_{L}-\mathbf{X}_{C}}{\mathbf{R}}\right] $$ When L is removed, then $ X_{L} = 0 $ $$ \phi=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{R}}\right) $$ $ \tan \phi = \left| \frac{X_c}{R} \right| $
$$ \phi=\frac{\pi}{3} $$
When C is removed. then $$ x_{c}=0 $$ $$ \phi=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathbf{X}_{L}}{\mathbf{R}}\right) $$ $$ \phi=\left(\frac{\mathbf{X}_{\perp}}{\mathbf{R}}\right) $$ From eq. (i) and eq. (ii), we get $$ \mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{C}} $$ $\therefore$ Impedance, $Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+(X_{L}-X_{C})^{2}}$ $$ Z=R $$ Power Factor, $$ \cos\phi=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{Z}}=1 $$ ∴ Power factor, $ \cos\phi=1 $. - For this, we know the formula, $$ \tan\phi=\frac{V_{L}-V_{C}}{V_{R}}=\frac{100-40}{80}=\frac{3}{4} $$ $$ \phi=37^{\circ} $$ ∴ Power factor = $ \cos\phi = \cos37^{\circ} $ $$ =0.8 $$ - Given, Inductance, $ L = 20 \, \text{mH} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{H} $ Capacitance. $$ C=100\mu F=100\times10^{-6}F $$ Resistance. $$ R=50\Omega $$ $$ \mathbf{V}=10\sin314t $$ and equation of emf is, $$ \mathbf{V}=\mathbf{V}_{0}\sin\omega t $$ Compare eq. (i) and (ii), we get $$ V_{0}=10\ V\ and\ \omega=314\ rad\ s^{-1} $$ Power of the AC circuit is, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{P}&=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}\cos\phi\\&=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{Z}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}\right)^{2}\mathrm{R}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{0}}{\sqrt{2}.Z}\right)^{2}.฿ $$ Impedance, $$ \begin{aligned}\boldsymbol{Z}&=\sqrt{\mathbf{R}^{2}+(\overline{\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{L}}-\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{C}}})^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{\mathbf{R}^{2}+\left(\omega\mathbf{L}-\frac{1}{\omega\mathbf{C}}\right)^{2}}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}=\sqrt{(50)^{2}+\left(314\times20\times10^{-3}-\frac{1}{314\times100\times10^{-6}}\right)^{2}}\\=\sqrt{2500+(-25.56)^{2}}\approx56\Omega\end{array} $$ Putting the value of $Z = 56\ \Omega$ in eq. (iii), we get $$ \mathbf{P}=\left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{2}\times56}\right)^{2}\times50 $$ $$ P=\frac{100}{2\times3136}\times50 $$ $$ \mathrm{P}=0.79\mathrm{~W} $$ ∴ Power loss = 0.79 W - According to the question, the power drawn is $$ P=V_{rms}\cdot I_{rms}=V_{rms}\cdot\frac{V_{rms}}{R} $$ $$ \Rightarrow\mathrm{~\boldmath~V~}_{\mathrm{r m s}}^{2}=\mathrm{P R} $$ When inductor is connected in series with the resistor, then power drawn is, $$ \mathbf{P}^{\prime}=\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}\ .I_{\mathrm{rms}}\cos\ \phi $$ where, $ \phi = \text{phase difference} $ ∴ $$ \mathrm{P}^{\prime}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}.\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{\mathrm{Z}}.\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{Z}} $$ $$ \frac{V_{rms}^{2}}{R}\cdot\frac{R^{2}}{Z^{2}} $$ $ \therefore $ $$ \mathrm{P}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{P}.\mathrm{R}^{2}}{\mathrm{Z}^{2}}=\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{Z}}\right)^{2} $$ - $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{P}&=\mathrm{V}_{\text{rms}}\ \mathrm{I}_{\text{rms}}\cos\phi\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{1}{2}\times\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\frac{1}{8}\mathrm{W}\end{aligned} $$ - Power = rate of work done in one complete cycle, $$ \mathbf{P}_{\omega}=\frac{\mathbf{W}}{\mathrm{T}} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}_{\omega}=\frac{(\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{I}_{0}\cos\phi)\mathrm{T}/2}{\mathrm{T}} $$ $$ \mathbf{P}_{\omega}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{I}_{0}\cos\phi}{2} $$ where, $ \cos \phi $ is called the power factor of an AC circuit. - $ X_L = 31\ \Omega, X_C = 25\ \Omega, R = 8\ \Omega $ ∴ Impedance of series LCR is $$ \mathbf{Z}=\sqrt{\mathbf{R}^{2}+(\mathbf{X}_{L}-\mathbf{X}_{C})^{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{8^{2}+(31-25)^{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{64+36} $$ $$ Z=\sqrt{100}=10\Omega $$ Power factor, $ \cos\phi=\frac{R}{Z}=\frac{8}{10}=0.8 $ - Power loss is given by, $$ \mathbf{P}=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}\cos\boldsymbol{\phi} $$ At resonance, current and voltage are in the same phase, that is $ \phi = 0 $. $ \therefore $ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{P}&=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}\cos0^{\circ}\\&=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}=\mathbf{I}^{2}\mathbf{R}\end{aligned} $$

title: Transformers
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Given, Primary voltage, $ V_{P} = 220 \, V $ Secondary voltage, $ V_{S} = 11,000 V $ Power. $$ \mathbf{P}=88\ \mathrm{W} $$ $$ \mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{P}}=\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{S}}=\mathbf{P} $$ $$ \mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{S}}=\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{S}}\mathbf{l}_{\mathbf{S}} $$ $$ I_{S}=\frac{P_{s}}{V_{s}} $$ $$ I_{S}=\frac{88W}{11000V} $$ $$ l_{S}=0.008A $$ $$ \mathrm{I_{S\delta}=8\ m A} $$
##### Related Theory The relationship between current voltage and number of turns in the primary and secondary coils of the transformer is given by, $$ \frac{I_{P}}{I_{S}}=\frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}=\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}}. $$ - Given: $$ \frac{\aleph_{p}}{\aleph_{s}}=1:2 $$ $$ \eta=\frac{\mathrm{V_{s}}}{\mathrm{V_{p}}}=\frac{\mathrm{N_{s}}}{\mathrm{N_{p}}} $$ ∴ $$ \mathrm{\frac{v_{s}}{v_{p}}}={\frac{2}{1}} $$ -
Let us consider current in the primary is $ I_{p} $. As the transformer is ideal $$ \begin{array}{r} \begin{aligned}\mathrm{P}_{i}&=\mathrm{P}_{0}\\ 220\times\mathrm{I}_{p}&=60\end{aligned}\end{array} $$ or $$ I_{p}=\frac{60}{220}=\frac{3}{11}=0.27A $$ - Given, step-down transformer $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}&=220\mathrm{~V}\\\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}}&=11\mathrm{~V}\\\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}}&=44\mathrm{~W}\\\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{P}}&=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}}\\\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}&=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}}=4\mathrm{~A}\\\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{P}}&=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}\\\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{P}}&=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}}=\frac{11\times4}{220}=\frac{4}{20}\\&=\frac{1}{5}=0.2\mathrm{~A}\end{aligned} $$ - According to the question, Efficiency = 90% = 0.9 Input voltage, $ V_{P} = 200 \, V $ Input power, $ P_{P} = 3000 $ W Secondary coil current, $ I_{S} = 6 \, A $ $$ \begin{aligned}V_{P}I_{P}&=3000\\ I_{P}&=\frac{3000}{V_{P}}=\frac{3000}{200}=15A\end{aligned} $$ $$ \therefore\text{Now efficiency}=\frac{\text{Output}}{\text{input}}=\frac{V_{S}I_{S}}{V_{P}I_{P}} $$ $$ 0.9=\frac{V_{s}\times6}{200\times15} $$ $$ \mathrm{V_{S}}=450~\mathrm{V} $$ ∴ Secondary coil current = 450 V. - Transformer can not work on DC. - Efficiency, $$ ↳\Downarrow $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\eta\%&=\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\times100=\frac{V_{s}i_{s}}{V_{p}i_{p}}\times100\\80&=\frac{2\times440}{220\times i_{p}}\times100\\i_{P}&=5A\end{aligned} $$ - According to the question, $$ \mathrm{N_{P}=50,N_{S}=1500} $$ and $$ \phi=\phi_{\mathrm{v}}+4\mathrm{t} $$ Voltage across primary coil, $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{d\phi}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}(\phi_{\mathrm{v}}+4\mathrm{t}) $$ $$ =4V $$ Now $$ \mathrm{\frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}}={\mathrm{\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}}}} $$ $$ V_{S}=\frac{1500}{50}\times4=120V $$ - The efficiency of transformer, $$ \begin{array}{l} \eta =\frac{P_S}{V_A I_P} \times 100 \\ =\frac{100}{220 \times 0.5} \times 100 \approx 90\% \end{array} $$ - The core of a transformers is laminated to minimise the energy losses due to eddy currents. - For step-up transformer $$ \frac{\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{P}}}\;=\;\frac{\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{P}}}\;=\;\frac{\mathbf{l}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathbf{l}_{\mathrm{S}}} $$ Here, $$ \mathrm{\frac{N_{p}}{N_{s}}} = \frac{1}{25} $$ or $$ l_{S}=2\ A $$ $$ \frac{1_{P}}{2}=\frac{25}{1} $$ or $$ l_{P}=25\times2=50A $$
##### Related Theory We have the change in flux of the primary and secondary coils is proportional to the number of turns in the primary and secondary coils, respectively. So, $$ \frac{\phi_{P}}{N_{P}}=\frac{\phi_{S}}{N_{S}} $$ or $$ \frac{1}{N_{P}}\times\frac{d\phi_{P}}{dt}=\frac{1}{N_{S}}\frac{d\phi_{S}}{dt} $$ $$ \frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}=\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}} $$ $$ \left(a s V\propto\frac{d\phi}{d t}\right) $$ If there is no power loss, $$ VI=constant $$ $$ I=\frac{1}{V}\times\mathrm{constant} $$ or $$ \frac{I_{P}}{I_{S}}=\frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}or\frac{I_{P}}{I_{S}}=\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}} $$ $I_{p}$ and $I_{s}$ are currents in primary and secondary coils respectively. - The transformer converts high-voltage AC current into low-voltage AC current without changing the frequency. The voltage across the input is divided by the voltage across the output. We have, The voltage is given by, $$ \mathrm{\frac{Vs}{V_{p}}}={\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}} $$ $ N_{S} $ = Number of turns in secondary coil $ N_{P}= $ Number of turns in primary coil $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{p}}}\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{P}} $$ Substitute the given values, we get $$ =\frac{5000}{500}\times20=200V $$ Thus, output has voltage 200 V and frequency 50 Hz.
2023年1月1日星期日

title: reNEET2026
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- $\omega=2\times10^3$. $X_L=\omega L=20\ \Omega$, $X_C=1/(\omega C)=20\ \Omega$; at resonance $Z=R=100\ \Omega$. $I_0=V_0/Z=220/100=2.2$ A.
